pending_diagnosis: DIAGNOSIS: Acute Intermittent Porphyria (AIP) THE SMOKING GUN: On review of the nursing notes prior to intubation, the patient received a broad-spectrum antibiotic (ceftriaxone) and diazepam early in her course. The key, however, was noted by a sharp-eyed resident who found the patient's urine sample, collected prior to transfer, had turned a distinctive, deep color upon standing, initially reported as "tea-colored." The lab confirmation: Grossly elevated Urinary Porphobilinogen (PBG) and Delta-Aminolevulinic Acid (ALA). PATHOPHYSIOLOGY: Acute Intermittent Porphyria is an autosomal dominant disorder caused by a partial deficiency of the enzyme Porphobilinogen Deaminase (PBGD), also known as Hydroxymethylbilane Synthase (HMBS), the third enzyme in the heme biosynthesis pathway. Attacks are typically triggered by specific drugs (e.g., barbiturates, sulfonamides, some anti-epileptics), alcohol, fasting, or hormonal changes. The initial medications given to Ms. R.K. (diazepam and ceftriaxone) were powerful inducers of the P450 system, accelerating heme synthesis and thus exacerbating the deficiency. The deficiency leads to the accumulation of highly neurotoxic precursors, specifically ALA and PBG, which target the central, peripheral, and autonomic nervous systems. The symptoms seen—autonomic hyperactivity (tachycardia, hypertension), profound abdominal pain (visceral neuropathy), hyponatremia (likely from inappropriate ADH secretion), and ascending motor neuropathy/psychosis—are pathognomonic of an acute porphyric crisis. Treatment involves immediate cessation of inducing drugs, high-dose glucose infusion, and most importantly, intravenous Heme Arginate (or Hematin) to downregulate ALA synthase and stop the production of the toxic precursors.
